title: Breaking the Silence: Redefining Your Personal Strength

sections.title: The Inherited Story
prompts: What messages did you receive growing up about showing struggle or asking for help? (e.g., 'men don't cry', 'figure it out yourself', 'don't be a burden')
Where did those messages come from — a parent, culture, a specific moment?
Write one belief about strength you absorbed that you now suspect was wrong.

sections.title: The Hidden Weight You're Carrying
prompts: Is there something you've been saying 'I'm fine' about that isn't fine? Write it here (no one else reads this).
How long have you been carrying it alone?
What has that silence cost you — in energy, relationships, sleep, or presence?

sections.title: The Double Standard Audit
prompts: Would you tell a close friend to 'just push through' the thing you're currently carrying? Why or why not?
You'd hire a trainer for physical strength without shame. List one mental or emotional area where hiring support (therapist, coach, counsellor) would make the same logical sense.
What's the real reason you haven't sought that support yet — social fear, cost, not knowing how, or something else?

sections.title: Making Space — For Yourself and Others
prompts: Think of someone in your life who seems 'off' lately. What's one low-pressure way you could check in on them this week?
Recall a time someone made space for you — even a small moment. What did they do that made it feel safe?
Write one sentence you could say to start an honest conversation with someone you trust about something you've been holding alone.

sections.title: Your Next Small Act
prompts: What is one concrete thing you will do in the next 7 days to chip at the wall — reach out to a friend, book a therapy consult, journal daily, or simply say 'I'm not okay' to one person?
Who is one specific person you could tell the truth to this week?
Finish this sentence: 'Real strength, for me, now looks like ___________'

cta: Share this worksheet with one person you think might need permission to stop carrying it alone — or use it yourself before this week ends. Struggle spoken out loud loses half its weight.
